title: Remove Nth Node From End
code: public ListNode RemoveNthFromEnd(ListNode head, int n)
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) fast = fast.next;
while (fast.next != null) { fast = fast.next; slow = slow.next; }

frames.caption: Put a dummy node in front of the head, then start both fast and slow at the dummy. We want to delete the n = 2nd node from the end (node 4).
frames.note: Counting from the tail is awkward without knowing the length. The trick: keep two pointers a fixed gap apart so that when the leader hits the end, the follower is exactly at the node we care about. The dummy in front means "remove the head" needs no special case — slow always has a valid predecessor to rewire.

frames.caption: Advance fast one step (i = 0). After this it leads slow by 1 node.
frames.note: Advance fast first so a fixed gap opens up between fast and slow. The reviewer's loop runs n steps; here we walk n+1 from the dummy so that when fast finally falls off the end (fast == null), slow lands on the PREDECESSOR of the target rather than the target itself — which is exactly what we need to splice the node out.

frames.caption: Advance fast one step (i = 1). After this it leads slow by 2 nodes.
frames.note: Advance fast first so a fixed gap opens up between fast and slow. The reviewer's loop runs n steps; here we walk n+1 from the dummy so that when fast finally falls off the end (fast == null), slow lands on the PREDECESSOR of the target rather than the target itself — which is exactly what we need to splice the node out.

frames.caption: Gap of n = 2 opened: fast is at node 2, slow still at the dummy. They are now 2 nodes apart.
frames.note: The gap is exactly n nodes wide. Hold this gap constant and the rest of the algorithm is just sliding the pair forward until the leader runs out of list.

frames.caption: fast.next is not null → step both forward together. fast = 3, slow = 1 (gap of 2 preserved).
frames.note: Now both pointers move in lockstep, preserving the constant gap. Because the gap never changes, the moment fast reaches the end, slow is pinned a fixed distance behind it — the predecessor of the nth-from-last node. No second pass and no length count are needed: the gap encodes "n from the end" for us.

frames.caption: fast.next is not null → step both forward together. fast = 4, slow = 2 (gap of 2 preserved).
frames.note: Now both pointers move in lockstep, preserving the constant gap. Because the gap never changes, the moment fast reaches the end, slow is pinned a fixed distance behind it — the predecessor of the nth-from-last node. No second pass and no length count are needed: the gap encodes "n from the end" for us.

frames.caption: fast.next is not null → step both forward together. fast = 5, slow = 3 (gap of 2 preserved).
frames.note: Now both pointers move in lockstep, preserving the constant gap. Because the gap never changes, the moment fast reaches the end, slow is pinned a fixed distance behind it — the predecessor of the nth-from-last node. No second pass and no length count are needed: the gap encodes "n from the end" for us.

frames.caption: fast.next == null → stop. fast is the last node (5); slow is at node 3, the predecessor of the target node 4.
frames.note: Loop exits precisely when the leader reaches the final node. Because the gap is n, slow lands one node before the nth-from-end — the node to delete is slow.next.

frames.caption: Splice it out: slow.next = slow.next.next, so node 3 now points past 4 to 5. Node 4 is dropped from the list.
frames.note: slow is the node just before the target, so slow.next = slow.next.next removes the target by routing the link past it (removeEdge the old link, addEdge to the successor). The dummy guarantees slow is always a real node even when the head itself is being removed, so this single line handles every case. Return dummy.next as the (possibly new) head.

frames.caption: Return dummy.next as the head. The list is now 1 → 2 → 3 → 5 — node 4 (the 2nd from the end) is gone, in a single pass.
frames.note: dummy.next is the real head whether or not the head changed, which is why the dummy removed the head-deletion special case. Total work: one forward pass to open the gap plus one to walk it down — O(n) time, O(1) extra space.
